title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on an already installed RHEL 9 system requires adding the dracut fips module and booting with the fips=1 kernel parameter. This change is not supported on existing installations, can render the system unbootable, and may break cryptographic services. Policy classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system unbootability; cryptographic subsystem impact

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding a kernel boot argument (fips=1) and setting the system cryptographic policy, which cannot be applied safely to an already installed system without a full reinstall. The change affects core cryptographic libraries, may break compatibility, and requires a reboot, making automated remediation too risky.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; kernel boot parameter modification; activation of FIPS mode; potential system instability; compatibility issues with existing software; mandatory reboot

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the 'fips=1' kernel boot parameter, which modifies the bootloader configuration and forces the system to operate under strict cryptographic constraints. This change is not supported on an already installed system and can render the system unbootable or cause incompatibilities with existing software. Therefore, remediation is considered too risky to perform without extensive testing and planning.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; bootloader modification; FIPS mode activation; potential system instability; unsupported on existing installations

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: The finding requires verification that the current RHEL 9 installation is still within Red Hat's support lifecycle. Remediation may involve an OS version upgrade or migration, which can impact system stability, compatibility, and require extensive testing. This is not a simple configuration change and therefore warrants human decision-making.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; potential OS upgrade; downtime during upgrade; compatibility with existing applications; possible need for reboot; impact on system stability

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing a plain text file (/etc/issue) to display the required legal disclaimer. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, network services, or boot configuration, and it will not lock out users or require a reboot. It is a straightforward, low‑impact configuration edit.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up /etc/issue, overwrites it, sets permissions, and attempts to fix the SELinux context. However, the disclaimer text supplied is incomplete and does not contain all mandatory DoD clauses required by the finding, so the compliance issue would likely remain.
review_concerns: The disclaimer text is truncated and omits several required statements (e.g., monitoring details, consent language, legal notices).; Using chcon with --reference may not restore the exact original SELinux context; restorecon is the typical and safer method.; The plan does not explicitly verify that the write operation succeeded before changing permissions.

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/password-auth File.
triage_reason: The remediation modifies PAM configuration (authselect or direct PAM file edits). Incorrect changes can lock out all users, including root, so human oversight is required despite being a password‑related lockout setting.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential account lockout; authentication failure; PAM misconfiguration; loss of system access

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/system-auth File.
triage_reason: The remediation edits PAM configuration via authselect or direct file modification. Incorrect settings can lock out legitimate users, potentially causing loss of access. Because it impacts authentication mechanisms, a manual review is required before applying.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential account lockout; Authentication failure for legitimate users; Loss of remote access if lockout thresholds are misconfigured

title: Lock Accounts After Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration to enable the pam_faillock module and set a deny count. This is a password‑policy related change (account lockout) which the policy classifies as safe to remediate. The provided script uses authselect when available, minimizing manual edits. While an incorrect deny value could lock out accounts, the change is low‑impact and can be validated before full deployment.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential account lockout if deny value is mis‑set; Dependency on authselect profile integrity; Incorrect PAM ordering could prevent logins
review_feedback: The plan only edits /etc/security/faillock.conf but does not ensure that the pam_faillock module is actually enabled in the PAM stack (system-auth, password-auth). Without the corresponding PAM entries, setting deny has no effect, so the vulnerability would remain on many systems.
review_concerns: Missing activation of pam_faillock in PAM configuration (authselect or manual edits).; Assumes the system already has the required PAM entries, which may not be true.; No backup of the original faillock.conf before modification.; No validation that the resulting file syntax is correct after sed operation.

title: Configure the root Account for Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation modifies PAM configuration to enforce a lockout after failed root login attempts. While it touches authentication settings, lockout policies are explicitly listed as safe password‑related rules. The change can be applied with authselect or a controlled edit of PAM files and does not require system reboot, making it low‑risk if performed carefully.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential root account lockout if PAM syntax is incorrect; Misconfiguration could prevent all logins
review_feedback: The overall approach (using authselect when available and falling back to manual edits) is sound, but the proposed pam_faillock configuration omits the 'even_deny_root' option required to lock the root account. Without this flag the root user will not be subject to the deny count, so the vulnerability remains. Additionally, the fallback sed edits do not guarantee correct line ordering or prevent duplication, and no backup is taken before changes.
review_concerns: Missing 'even_deny_root' parameter in pam_faillock lines, so root may not be locked out.; Manual sed edits could insert lines in the wrong order or duplicate existing entries.; No backup of PAM files before applying changes.; No explicit verification that the pam_faillock module is present on the system.

title: Set Interval For Counting Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The finding requires adding or updating a single PAM configuration directive (fail_interval) using authselect, which is a standard, low‑impact change. While it touches authentication mechanisms, the policy classifies lockout‑related settings as password‑policy items that are safe to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for account lockout if interval is set incorrectly; Misconfiguration could affect PAM authentication flow
remedy_commands_executed: authselect enable-feature with-faillock; authselect apply-changes; cp /etc/security/faillock.conf /etc/security/faillock.conf.bak
review_feedback: The plan will set the required fail_interval value and uses authselect for PAM configuration, addressing the vulnerability. Minor improvements can make it more robust.
review_concerns: Directly editing /etc/security/faillock.conf may be overwritten by future authselect profile changes.; The backup and edit steps assume the file exists; if it does not, the backup command will fail.; The use of 'sed -r' is GNU‑specific; on non‑GNU systems the option may be '-E'.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses authselect, the recommended tool for managing PAM on Rocky Linux 10, to enable the with‑faillock feature and then safely edits /etc/security/faillock.conf to set fail_interval. These actions are standard, low‑risk, and do not replace existing PAM files, so critical services are unlikely to be disrupted.
qa_side_effects: Creation of a backup file /etc/security/faillock.conf.bak; Potential overwriting of the fail_interval setting on future authselect profile changes; If /etc/security/faillock.conf does not exist, the backup command will fail (non‑critical); Minor risk of temporary authentication issues if the pam_faillock module is mis‑enabled

title: Set Lockout Time for Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The finding modifies a PAM lockout parameter, which is classified as a password‑policy setting. According to policy, lockout‑related PAM changes are safe to remediate automatically. The remediation uses authselect, a supported tool, minimizing risk of misconfiguration.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for account lockout if unlock_time is set incorrectly; Incorrect authselect profile could cause PAM misconfiguration
review_feedback: The plan only updates /etc/security/faillock.conf but does not ensure the required pam_faillock entries are present in the PAM stack. The official remediation uses authselect (or authconfig) to enable the 'with-faillock' feature, which updates system-auth and password-auth accordingly. Without those changes the rule may still be non‑compliant even though unlock_time is set.
review_concerns: Missing authselect/authconfig steps to enable pam_faillock in PAM configuration.; Potential for duplicate or malformed entries if the file already contains multiple unlock_time lines.; Direct file editing bypasses the recommended tool (authselect), increasing risk of configuration drift.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The finding concerns a password complexity setting (dcredit) in the PAM pwquality module. Adjusting this parameter only changes password composition requirements and does not affect authentication mechanisms or system boot. According to policy, password policy changes are classified as safe to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user inconvenience if the new requirement is too strict; Possible lockout for existing passwords that do not meet the new digit requirement
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*#\?[[:space:]]*dcredit[[:space:]]*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo 'dcredit = -1' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set dcredit=-1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which satisfies the requirement to require at least one digit. It correctly handles existing commented or uncommented entries and adds the setting if missing. However, it does not address possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf and does not create a backup before editing. Adding those steps would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: Potential existing dcredit entries in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ could override the change made in pwquality.conf.; No backup of the original configuration file is created before modification.; No check of the sed command's exit status to confirm the edit succeeded.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting dcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf correctly enforces at least one digit in new passwords and does not alter PAM modules or system services. The change is limited to password quality configuration and will not break critical services, though it may cause password change operations that do not meet the new rule to fail.
qa_side_effects: Password changes that do not include at least one digit will be rejected, possibly affecting automated scripts that set passwords.; If a conflicting dcredit setting exists in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/, the effective policy may be ambiguous.; No backup of the original file is created, so accidental mis‑edit cannot be easily reverted.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Prevent the Use of Dictionary Words
triage_reason: The finding concerns enabling the 'dictcheck' option of pam_pwquality, which is a password‑policy setting. Adjusting this does not modify authentication mechanisms themselves, only adds an extra password strength check, and is unlikely to lock out legitimate users.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential rejection of passwords containing dictionary words; User inconvenience if passwords are too simple
remedy_commands_executed: ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i -E 's/^\s*#?\s*dictcheck\s*=.*/dictcheck = 1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan now creates a backup of the main configuration, updates any existing dictcheck entries in pwquality.conf.d files, and ensures the setting is present in the main file when absent. This will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. Minor refinements (preserving comment documentation, backing up .d files, and checking directory existence) would improve safety but are not required for the fix to work.
review_concerns: The sed command also replaces commented dictcheck lines, potentially discarding helpful documentation.; No backup is made for files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ before editing.; The script assumes the .d directory exists and does not explicitly verify it.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies pwquality configuration to enable dictcheck, which only influences password quality checks during password changes. It does not alter PAM stack order, services, or networking. The changes are limited to configuration files and include a backup of the main file, so the risk of breaking critical services or locking out SSH is negligible.
qa_side_effects: Password change operations may be rejected if the new password contains dictionary words; Original comments/documentation for dictcheck may be overwritten in edited files; No backup is made for files under /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ (possible loss of custom settings if edited incorrectly)

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Characters
triage_reason: The finding modifies only the PAM pwquality 'difok' setting, which controls password character difference requirements. This is a standard password policy adjustment that does not affect authentication mechanisms beyond password complexity and does not risk locking out users if set to a reasonable value.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Setting difok too high may cause user password change failures; Incorrect syntax could prevent pwquality from loading
remedy_commands_executed: cat /etc/authselect/authselect.conf
review_feedback: Review output was not valid JSON. Raw: { "finding_id": "accounts_password_pam_difok", "is_optimal": true, "approve": true, "feedback": "The plan uses authselect to modify the PAM configuration, which is the recommended way to manag...
review_concerns: Could not parse LLM response

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only adds the 'enforce_for_root' setting to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a straightforward PAM configuration change that enforces password complexity for the root account. This aligns with standard password policy hardening and does not disable authentication, so the risk of locking out access is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Root password must meet new complexity requirements; Potential need to update root password after remediation
review_feedback: Review output was not valid JSON. Raw: ...
review_concerns: Could not parse LLM response

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters from Same Character Class
triage_reason: The finding concerns only the pam_pwquality maxclassrepeat setting, which is a simple configuration change to enforce password complexity. Adjusting this parameter does not affect system authentication mechanisms beyond password validation and carries minimal risk of locking out users.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential rejection of passwords that exceed the new limit; User inconvenience if the limit is set too low
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; sed -i '/maxclassrepeat/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; printf '\nmaxclassrepeat = 2\n' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the maxclassrepeat parameter to enforce the required password policy. It will resolve the vulnerability if executed. However, it could be improved by handling potential duplicate entries, backing up the configuration file before modification, and considering any overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf.
review_concerns: The script may create duplicate 'maxclassrepeat' lines if the setting already exists elsewhere in the file.; It does not backup the original pwquality.conf before editing, which could complicate rollback.; It ignores possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ that might take precedence over the main file.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting maxclassrepeat = 2 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf only tightens password quality checks; it does not alter PAM stack logic or affect existing authentication, so critical services will continue to operate. The change is limited to a single parameter and is reversible.
qa_side_effects: If a duplicate maxclassrepeat entry already exists, the file may contain multiple lines, which could cause ambiguous parsing (though PAM typically uses the last value).; Users will be required to meet the stricter password rule when changing passwords, which could cause password change failures for weak passwords.

title: Set Password Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the pam_pwquality maxrepeat setting, which affects password composition rules without impacting authentication flow or system services. This change is low risk and unlikely to lock out users.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; users may need to adjust passwords to meet the new rule
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; grep -R "maxrepeat" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d || true; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*maxrepeat\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo 'maxrepeat = 2' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly targets the pwquality configuration and sets maxrepeat = 2, which will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. However, the implementation can be made more robust.
review_concerns: The sed command only replaces commented lines; an existing uncommented maxrepeat directive would not be updated, potentially leaving an incorrect setting.; The plan modifies only /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, which could cause the setting to be overridden.; If a maxrepeat line already exists and is uncommented, the script could append a duplicate entry, leading to ambiguous configuration.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing only the pwquality maxrepeat parameter modifies password‑creation policy; it does not affect login authentication or critical daemons. The change is limited to /etc/security/pwquality.conf and will not break sshd, firewalld, networking, or other core services. The main risk is a possible duplicate or missed update if an existing uncommented line exists or if a .conf.d file overrides the setting, but this would only affect future password changes, not current access.
qa_side_effects: If an existing uncommented maxrepeat line is present, the sed command will not replace it, potentially leaving the old value unchanged.; Appending the directive when a maxrepeat line already exists could create duplicate entries, leading to ambiguous configuration.; Overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf may still take precedence, making the change ineffective.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Categories
triage_reason: The finding concerns only the PAM pwquality 'minclass' setting, which adjusts password complexity without affecting authentication flow or system boot. Changing this parameter is a standard, low‑risk remediation for password policy compliance.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user inconvenience if the new class requirement is too strict; Possible failure of password change scripts that assume fewer classes
remedy_commands_executed: ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; sed -i 's/^\s*#\s*minclass\s*=.*/minclass = 4/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set minclass to 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which satisfies the requirement. However, it only modifies the main file and does not consider possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, nor does it handle the case where the setting is absent or already active. Adding a backup, verifying existence, and ensuring idempotent addition would improve reliability.
review_concerns: Only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf; other configuration fragments in pwquality.conf.d may override the setting.; If the line is not commented but absent, the sed command will not add the required setting.; No backup of the configuration file before modification.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting minclass to 4 via a simple in‑place sed edit of /etc/security/pwquality.conf is a standard way to enforce stricter password complexity. It does not modify PAM stack logic, so existing authentication will continue to work with current passwords. The change only affects future password changes, which may inconvenience some users but will not cause service outages or lock out SSH access. The only minor risk is that other pwquality.conf.d fragments could override the setting, but that does not introduce instability.
qa_side_effects: Users may be forced to create passwords meeting all four character classes on next password change, possibly leading to failed password updates if they do not meet the criteria.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the pam_pwquality configuration to set a minimum password length. This is a straightforward file edit that does not affect system boot, kernel parameters, or critical authentication mechanisms beyond password complexity, and it is unlikely to lock out legitimate users.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential user inconvenience due to stricter password length; misconfiguration could prevent password changes if set incorrectly
remedy_commands_executed: test -d /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d && echo exists || echo missing; find /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d -type f -name "*.conf"; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-custom.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-custom.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[#\s]*minlen\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-custom.conf; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[#\s]*minlen\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -n "minlen" -R /etc/security/pwquality.conf; printf "minlen = 12\n" >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly enforces a minimum password length by cleaning existing minlen entries, backing up files, and adding a dedicated configuration file, which will remediate the vulnerability. Minor improvements are possible (e.g., parameterising the length and tightening the validation step).
review_concerns: The minimum length value (12) is hard‑coded rather than configurable.; The validation grep pattern also matches commented lines, which could report multiple matches even after remediation.; The plan assumes a ‘write_file’ utility; ensure it preserves SELinux contexts and correct ownership.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan cleanly removes any existing minlen settings, backs up files, and adds a dedicated configuration file with minlen=12. This is the correct remediation for the PAM pwquality requirement and does not modify authentication mechanisms beyond password strength, so it will not lock out users or disrupt critical services.
qa_side_effects: Potential SELinux context changes on backup files created with cp; Existing password change workflows that expect a different minlen value may need to be updated

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Authentication Retry Prompts Permitted Per-Session in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
triage_reason: The finding concerns adjusting the 'retry' parameter in pwquality.conf, a standard password policy setting. Changing this value is a simple configuration edit that does not risk locking out users and aligns with typical STIG remediation practices for password complexity and retry limits.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo 'NO_DIR'; sed -i '/retry/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; printf '\nretry = 3\n' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -R "retry" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; grep -n "^retry" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set the required retry value and thus satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. It could be strengthened by handling potential duplicate settings, backing up the original file, and considering any overriding configuration files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/.
review_concerns: The script does not address possible retry entries in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf which could override the main file.; If multiple 'retry' lines exist, the sed command will only modify the first matching line, leaving duplicates that might cause confusion.; No backup of the original /etc/security/pwquality.conf is created before in‑place editing.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting the pwquality retry parameter to a reasonable value (3) via editing /etc/security/pwquality.conf is unlikely to disrupt authentication or critical services; the change only affects password‑quality retry prompts during password changes.
qa_side_effects: If a retry line already exists, the unconditional append step could create duplicate entries.; The script does not backup the original configuration file.; Entries in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf are not handled and could override the setting.

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Remediation requires reviewing and potentially deleting local user accounts, which can inadvertently remove required accounts or lock out legitimate users, necessitating human verification and coordination.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential account lockout; service disruption; unauthorized deletion of critical accounts

title: Prevent Login to Accounts With Empty Password
triage_reason: The remediation edits PAM configuration (authselect profile or /etc/pam.d files) to remove the 'nullok' option. Incorrect changes to PAM can prevent all users from authenticating, potentially locking out administrators. Therefore a human review is required before applying.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential account lockout; authentication failure; system access disruption

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file mode bits on user initialization files (e.g., .bashrc, .profile) to 0740, removing group/other read/write/execute permissions. This does not modify authentication mechanisms, network settings, or bootloader configuration. While overly restrictive permissions could affect users who rely on group access, the change is reversible and unlikely to lock out access. Therefore it is considered a low‑to‑medium risk remediation that can be automated safely.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential disruption of user environment if group read access was required; Incorrect path handling could affect non‑user files
remedy_commands_executed: bash -c 'while IFS=: read -r user _ uid _ _ home shell; do if [ "$uid" -eq 0 ] || [ "$uid" -ge 1000 ]; then case "$user" in nobody|nfsnobody) continue;; esac; if [[ "$shell" != *nologin && "$shell" != *false && -d "$home" ]]; then for f in .bashrc .bash_profile .profile .bash_logout .cshrc .tcshrc .kshrc .zshrc .zprofile .zlogin .zshenv .login; do if [ -f "$home/$f" ]; then chmod 0740 "$home/$f"; fi; done; fi; fi; done < /etc/passwd'
review_feedback: The plan correctly targets user initialization files and sets the required 0740 mode, which would remediate the finding once fixed. However, the current awk command uses double quotes, causing variable expansion errors, and the loop does not handle home directories with spaces or exclude ignored system users. Adjusting these issues will make the remediation reliable.
review_concerns: Awk program is quoted with double quotes, leading to $3 expansion and a syntax error.; The for-loop over $(awk ...) does not handle home directory paths containing spaces.; Ignored users (e.g., nobody, nfsnobody) are not excluded, potentially modifying files that are not part of the scope.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only changes file permission bits on user init files and does not modify configuration or binaries. Changing to 0740 is more restrictive and will not affect critical services such as sshd, auditd, or networking. The only risk is that the script may not run as intended due to quoting errors and may affect system accounts unintentionally, but these actions would not cause service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Potential failure to locate user home directories because of incorrect awk quoting, resulting in no changes being applied; Possible handling issues for home directory paths that contain spaces, which could lead to incorrect chmod targets; System accounts (e.g., nobody) are not explicitly excluded, though changing their init files to 0740 is unlikely to break services

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/profile and scripts in /etc/profile.d to set a consistent umask value. This is a straightforward configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, networking, or boot processes, and it carries minimal risk of locking out users. The change can be applied safely with a backup and verification step.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential for overly permissive default file permissions if umask is set incorrectly; Misconfiguration could affect newly created files for all users
remedy_commands_executed: sed -i -E 's/^([[:space:]]*)umask([[:space:]]+)([0-7]+)/\1# umask\2\3/' /etc/profile
review_feedback: The plan will effectively enforce the required umask by commenting out existing settings in /etc/profile and adding a dedicated script in /etc/profile.d, addressing the vulnerability. Minor issues in the sed command and lack of explicit privilege notes should be corrected.
review_concerns: The sed replacement pattern does not capture the numeric umask value, resulting in a comment without the original number.; No explicit statement that the script must be run with root privileges; it will fail otherwise.; Only /etc/profile is backed up; existing umask scripts in /etc/profile.d are not backed up before modification (though they are not modified here).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly backs up /etc/profile, neutralizes any existing umask lines, and enforces the desired umask via a dedicated script in /etc/profile.d, which is the standard method. It does not modify system services or authentication mechanisms and therefore is unlikely to cause outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: The original numeric umask value in /etc/profile will be stripped from the commented line due to the sed replacement, but this does not affect functionality.; If other /etc/profile.d scripts also set umask, the new zz-umask.sh will override them, which may change file permission defaults for login shells.

triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; bootloader misconfiguration; potential system lockout; unbootable system; requires console access for recovery

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: The finding requires adding or verifying at least two 'nameserver' entries in /etc/resolv.conf, a simple text file edit. This change does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem structures and poses minimal risk of system lockout. title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to set a maxpoll value and restarts the time service. This is a straightforward, low‑impact change that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and it does not require a system reboot.

remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q chrony; rpm -q ntp; ls -1 /etc/chrony.d 2>/dev/null || true; which chronyc; chronyc checkconf /etc/chrony.conf; grep -n "\bserver\b" -n /etc/chrony.conf; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak.$(date +%s); perl -i -pe 'next if /^\s*#/; if (/^(.*\b(?:server|pool|peer)\b.*?)(\s*#.*)?$/) { my $pre=$1; my $cmt=$2 // ""; if ($pre =~ /maxpoll\s+\d+/) { $pre =~ s/maxpoll\s+\d+/maxpoll 10/; } else { $pre .= " maxpoll 10"; } $_ = $pre . $cmt . "\n"; }' /etc/chrony.conf; which perl; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak.$(date +%s); sed -i -E '/^\s*[^#]*\b(server|pool|peer)\b/ s/(maxpoll[[:space:]]+)[0-9]+/\1 10/g' /etc/chrony.conf; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak.$(date +%s); systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan adds backups, correctly modifies server/pool/peer lines to include or replace a maxpoll value before any trailing comment, and restarts the appropriate time service. It addresses the earlier sed issues and the comment‑placement problem. Adding a syntax validation step and making the maxpoll value configurable would further improve robustness.

This change does not affect critical services like sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking, and the risk of a service outage is limited to the time service, which is non‑critical for immediate system operation.
qa_side_effects: Creation of backup files in the same directory; Temporary restart of chronyd or ntpd, causing a brief loss of time synchronization; Potential service start failure if the edited config becomes syntactically invalid

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The finding requires editing the Chrony configuration to retain only 'server' directives. This is a simple file edit and service restart, with no impact on authentication, authorization, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and it does not risk locking out access.
triage_estimated_impact: Incorrect time source configuration could cause time drift; Potential service disruption if Chrony fails to start after edit
remedy_commands_executed: cp -p /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -e 's/^[[:space:]]*pool[[:space:]]/# &/' -e 's/^[[:space:]]*peer[[:space:]]/# &/' /etc/chrony.conf; echo 'server 0.rhel.pool.ntp.org iburst' >> /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl reload chronyd; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up the configuration, comments out any non‑server time source directives, ensures a server entry exists, and reloads the chronyd service. It will bring the system into compliance with the requirement to use only 'server' directives.
review_concerns: If the system does not support 'systemctl reload chronyd', the changes may not be applied; a fallback to restart could be needed.; Appending a default server line when no active 'server' directive exists could result in duplicate entries if a commented server line is later uncommented.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits /etc/chrony.conf, comments out pool/peer lines, ensures a server line exists, backs up the file, and reloads chronyd. These changes are limited to the NTP service, unlikely to affect critical services or cause lockouts, and address the vulnerability directly.
qa_side_effects: Potential temporary loss of additional time sources (pool/peer) leading to less accurate time until sync completes; If chronyd does not support 'systemctl reload', the configuration may not be applied until a restart; Appending a default server line could create duplicate server entries if one already exists but is commented

title: Uninstall tftp-server Package
triage_reason: Removing the tftp-server package is a low‑risk operation that does not modify authentication, authorization, bootloader, or critical filesystem settings. The primary concern is potential breakage of packages that depend on tftp-server, which can be verified before removal. According to the policy, low‑risk package removals are classified as safe to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: Possible dependent packages may be removed; Systems that rely on TFTP for file transfer could lose functionality
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q --quiet "tftp-server" && echo installed || echo not_installed; dnf remove -y --noautoremove "tftp-server"
review_feedback: The plan correctly removes the tftp-server package and will satisfy the OpenSCAP requirement. It includes verification of installation and uses non‑interactive removal, which will resolve the vulnerability.
review_concerns: Using '--noautoremove' may leave dependent packages installed that could become broken or unused, potentially causing system inconsistencies.; No explicit handling of removal errors or logging of the operation outcome.
qa_verdict_reason: Removing the optional tftp-server package does not impact core system services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The command correctly checks for installation and uses --noautoremove to avoid cascading removals, so the risk of breaking critical functionality is minimal. Any dependent packages will remain installed but may be non‑functional, which is acceptable for this remediation.
qa_side_effects: Potential leftover packages that depend on tftp-server may become broken or unusable; TFTP service will no longer be available

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling certmap modifies SSSD authentication mapping. Incorrect configuration can prevent users from authenticating, potentially locking out access. Therefore a manual review is required before applying changes.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; authentication lockout; certificate mapping errors; service disruption

title: Enable Smartcards in SSSD
triage_reason: The remediation edits PAM configuration files and SSSD settings, which are critical to authentication. Incorrect changes can lock out users or break smartcard authentication, so a human must verify the changes and test them before deployment.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential authentication lockout; PAM misconfiguration; smartcard dependency failure; service restart impact

title: Enable auditd Service
triage_reason: Enabling the auditd service is a low‑risk operation that only starts a system service and ensures it persists across boots. It does not modify authentication, networking, or filesystem structures and cannot lock out access. The remediation script is straightforward and safe to apply on RHEL 9.
triage_estimated_impact: audit records not being collected; potential compliance gaps
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q kernel-core; rpm -q audit; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl unmask auditd.service; systemctl start auditd.service; systemctl enable auditd.service
review_feedback: The plan correctly enables auditd by unmasking, starting, and enabling the service. Adding a check that the system is not in an offline state before attempting to start the daemon, and verifying that the required packages are installed before proceeding, would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: The start command may fail if systemd is in an offline or degraded state.; The plan does not explicitly verify that the audit package is installed before attempting to enable the service.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only unmasks, starts, and enables auditd using systemctl after confirming package presence. Enabling auditd is a standard remediation and does not interfere with SSH, networking, or other critical services; worst‑case is increased audit logging load.
qa_side_effects: Increased audit logging may consume additional CPU and disk space; If audit rules are misconfigured, auditd could generate excessive logs

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the permission bits of the audit log file to a more restrictive mode (0600). This does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes, and auditd runs as root so it will retain read/write access. The change is straightforward and low‑risk.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential audit daemon write failure if permissions are set incorrectly; Log file may be in use during permission change
remedy_commands_executed: awk -F '=' '/^[[:space:]]*log_file[[:space:]]*=/ {gsub(/[[:space:]"\']/, "", $2); print $2}' /etc/audit/auditd.conf; stat -c '%a' /var/log/audit/audit.log; chmod 0600 /var/log/audit/audit.log
review_feedback: The plan will set the audit log file permissions to 0600, which satisfies the requirement of 0640 or more restrictive. It correctly parses the auditd.conf to locate the log file and applies the restrictive mode. Minor enhancements are recommended for robustness and compliance with SELinux contexts.
review_concerns: The placeholder <file_path> must be replaced with the actual resolved path before execution.; Creating the audit log file if it does not exist could result in incorrect SELinux context or ownership, potentially interfering with auditd.; The plan does not verify that the audit package and auditd service are present before making changes.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly determines the audit log location, ensures the file exists, and applies a 0600 mode which is more restrictive than the required 0640. auditd runs as root and can write to a 0600 file, so core services remain unaffected. The only minor concern is creating the file manually may set a default SELinux context, but this is unlikely to disrupt auditd operation.
qa_side_effects: If the audit log file does not exist, creating an empty file may apply a default SELinux context that differs from the expected audit_log_t type.; Creating an empty audit log file could temporarily affect log rotation until the proper context is restored.
